next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 15

Fasa had not slept.

She sat in the custodians' quarters at the low stone table where the radio rested in its leather case, the lamp turned down to a thread of light that barely pushed back the dark corners of the room. Through the wall she could hear the compound settling — someone shifting on a mat, the rustle of a blanket drawn up against the night's chill, and farther out, the flood's steady rush from the valley floor, and the rain, lighter than it had been in weeks, falling on the stone overhang in irregular clusters.

She had watched Fen return to Gitta.

She had stood in the doorway of the main hall as the boy came back in the small hours, his face drawn tight, and sat down beside the old woman without a word. Gitta had not moved. Not to reach for him, not to speak, not to touch his arm. She had sat with the absolute stillness that Fasa had come to recognize as the woman's deepest register — not the stillness of someone trying to be still but the stillness of someone who had spent sixteen years holding herself in place. Fasa had seen many things in twenty years of the naming. Refusals and tears and fury and a man who had thrown a clay cup against the wall and split his own lip on the shards. She had never seen anything as quiet as what passed between those two bodies. The boy choosing the woman who had stolen his story, choosing her by sitting close enough that their shoulders nearly touched. The woman accepting that closeness without reaching for more.

Fasa had gone back to her quarters after that. She had boiled water in the iron pot and poured tea i...
open_hooks_to_carry: Gitta asking to speak at the naming — the silence before she begins
the confession: the dead family on the road, the living infant, the lie told to Miro and Enn, the name she gave him
prev_tail_excerpt:
...iew of the courtyard and its standing water. Habel followed. The other Kondo children trailed after, subdued, wide-eyed.

Arash was not among them.

Venn stood in the hall and looked at the empty space where his son had been sitting, and then at the doorway through which the Daos had disappeared, and he did not go looking. He did not need to. He knew where Arash was with the same certainty he knew his own name — not because he had proof but because the evidence had been accumulating for days, in Arash's morning absences and evening distances and the careful blankness he showed when Venn looked at him, and the confrontation with Bett had not caused it but had made it impossible to keep pretending he didn't see.

His family was coming apart. Not the family he administered — the twelve Kondos traveling to a wedding that now seemed as distant and as abstract as the capital — but the idea that the family was all there was, that the Kondos began and ended with the Kondos, that the walls he had spent twenty-two years building were walls rather than lines he had drawn in the dust and called permanent.

He stood alone in the main hall of the transit shrine. The oil lamps guttered. The rain fell. The flood, indifferent and immovable below them in the valley, made its low unending sound. Somewhere in the sleeping quarters Ori whimpered, and the sound carried through the thin stone walls the way all sounds carried in this place — as if the shrine had been designed to make sure nothing stayed private, nothing stayed contained, nothing remained the property of any single family for long.
state_deltas_required: Gitta's sixteen-year secret about Fen's origins becomes unsustainable after the rupture

the naming produces its deepest revelation; Fen's identity is unmade and remade; the true kinship map reframes as a history of displacement and chosen bonds; Jin witnesses and feels his own lie tremble
